title: US Opens AI Chip Floodgates to UAE While China Builds Reusable Rockets
subhead: The Commerce Department loosened Nvidia export controls for the UAE, SK Hynix debuted on Nasdaq with the largest foreign listing in US history, and China successfully recovered its first reusable orbital rocket — a week that rewired the maps for AI compute, chip markets, and space access.

sections.heading: US Eases AI Chip Export Controls for UAE
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department moved the United Arab Emirates into a more favorable export control grouping on July 10, making it licence-free for approved companies to import Nvidia AI chips, military equipment, commercial satellites, and spacecraft. The change, published in the Federal Register, places the UAE alongside NATO countries and close allies — the only country in the group that is not a member of multilateral export control regimes. Israel and Saudi Arabia, by contrast, are not in this grouping.

Eight US-headquartered companies — Amazon, Apple, Google, Meta, Microsoft, OpenAI, Oracle, and xAI — can now bring advanced computing items into the UAE without individual export licenses. UAE-based firms G42 and Core42 also gained licence-free access. The Commerce Department said it will 'favourably review' export license applications for chips and servers to UAE company MGX. The move follows a preliminary 2025 agreement allowing the UAE to import hundreds of thousands of Nvidia AI chips.

The Commerce Department explicitly linked the decision to the UAE's role in 'Operation Epic Fury' — the US-Israeli strikes on Iran — and noted that UAE foreign direct investment in the US exceeds $1 trillion. A former Commerce official noted that under the new regime, 'there will no longer be room for debate within the administration' over chip licenses to firms like G42, which had been contentious due to potential Chinese customer exposure.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is trading AI chip access for geopolitical alignment. The UAE supported US military operations against Iran and is being rewarded with a near-unrestricted pipeline to advanced Nvidia GPUs — putting it ahead of regional rivals like Saudi Arabia and Israel in the race to build domestic AI compute capacity.

sections.heading: OpenAI and Google Sold AI Models to Pentagon-Blacklisted Chinese Firms
sections.body:
A Financial Times investigation revealed that OpenAI and Google have been selling their advanced AI models to Singapore-based subsidiaries of Alibaba, Baidu, and Tencent — Chinese tech giants that appear on the Pentagon's 1260H blacklist for alleged military ties. Both companies confirmed the transactions, which exploit a legal gap: Singapore-incorporated entities can contract with American AI providers even when their parent companies are blacklisted.

The Pentagon expanded its blacklist in June 2026 to include additional Chinese firms. The FT report highlights how the US regulatory framework for AI model exports lags behind semiconductor controls. While chips are subject to strict export licensing, frontier AI models accessed via API are not comparably restricted, creating what the report describes as a significant gap in the US-China technology controls architecture.
sections.whyItMatters: US export controls are effective at throttling chip flows but largely blind to AI model access. As long as a Chinese firm can route through a Singapore subsidiary, frontier AI capabilities remain accessible — undermining the premise that Washington can restrict China's AI advancement through trade controls alone.

sections.heading: China Flies and Recovers Its First Reusable Orbital Rocket
sections.body:
On July 10, China's Long March 10B completed its maiden flight from the Wenchang Commercial Space Launch Site on Hainan Island, then successfully recovered its first-stage booster using a novel midair net-catch system on an offshore vessel in the South China Sea. The China Aerospace Science and Technology Corporation (CASC) hailed the flight as a 'complete success.'

The Long March 10B is a medium-lift rocket with approximately 16 metric tons to low-Earth orbit, slightly less than SpaceX's Falcon 9. Instead of propulsive landing, the booster descended to an offshore vessel where tensioned cables in a grid pattern captured the rocket as it shut down its engines — a technique similar to SpaceX's Mechazilla tower catch but adapted for sea-based recovery. The upper stage deployed a payload designated CX-26.

CASC said the first-stage reuse flight test is expected by the end of this year. The Long March 10 family is central to China's lunar ambitions: a heavier three-booster configuration is planned to carry Chinese astronauts to the Moon by 2030. China is the world's second-largest spacefaring nation but launches at roughly half the cadence of the US, a gap driven largely by SpaceX's reusability advantage. Multiple Chinese commercial and government rocket companies are now racing to close it.
sections.whyItMatters: China just demonstrated it can recover and reuse orbital-class boosters — the capability that underpins SpaceX's launch dominance. If CASC achieves rapid reuse turnaround, it narrows the launch cadence gap that currently gives the US a significant advantage in deploying satellite constellations and sustaining orbital infrastructure.

sections.heading: China-Russia Leaked Slides Detail Plans to Counter Starlink
sections.body:
A joint investigation by The Insider, Der Spiegel, and Le Monde published leaked presentation slides from the Third China-Russia Military-Technical Cooperation Forum in Guangzhou, showing a detailed CASC-authored briefing on countering SpaceX's Starlink constellation. The slides, classified as 'internal' (one level above public release), were authored by CASC representatives Huang Hui and Ren Jie.

The presentation outlines three categories of countermeasures: physical destruction of Starlink satellites (with emphasis on cost-effectiveness, including small pellets designed to destroy solar panels across multiple satellites), electromagnetic jamming, and cyber attacks that would upload viruses from Starlink end terminals to paralyze the network. A NATO source told The Insider that the alliance has been tracking similar proposals, including one using small pellets to destroy satellite solar panels at scale.

A separate slide advocates a 'security alliance' between Beijing and Moscow with a layered diplomatic strategy: using regulatory pressure to slow Starlink deployments by citing collision risks, challenging SpaceX's frequency and orbital slot allocations through international regulation, and forming a 'technological alliance' to 'reshape a new strategic balance with the United States.' The presentation also reveals broader military cooperation including joint air defense systems and technology exchanges — going beyond previously reported joint drills.
sections.whyItMatters: This is not a theoretical exercise. The leaked slides show China's state aerospace corporation actively designing countermeasures against a system that is Ukraine's primary military communications backbone — and proposing a formal alliance framework with Russia to do so. The plans for physical destruction and cyber attacks on commercial satellite constellations represent a direct escalation in the militarization of orbital space.

sections.heading: EU Declares Meta's Addictive Design Illegal Under Digital Services Act
sections.body:
The European Commission found that Facebook and Instagram's 'addictive design' features violate the Digital Services Act, ordering Meta to make significant changes to both platforms. The Commission said certain design features can cause users' brains to go into 'autopilot mode,' including infinite scroll, notifications designed to maximize engagement, and algorithmic curation that keeps users on-platform beyond their intent.

This is the latest escalation in Brussels' enforcement of the DSA against major platforms, and one of the most significant: it targets not content moderation failures but the fundamental design architecture of two of the world's largest social media products. Meta will need to either remove or fundamentally restructure these features for EU users or face substantial fines.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU is moving from regulating what platforms publish to regulating how platforms are built. If upheld, this ruling forces a structural redesign of the engagement-maximizing features that drive Meta's advertising revenue — and sets a precedent other jurisdictions will study closely.

sections.heading: SK Hynix Debuts on Nasdaq in Largest Foreign Listing in US History
sections.body:
South Korean memory chipmaker SK Hynix completed its Nasdaq listing on July 10, marking the largest public listing by a foreign company in US market history. The listing is explicitly positioned as a bet that the AI boom has broken the traditional boom-and-bust cycle of the memory chip industry.

SK Hynix is the dominant supplier of HBM (high-bandwidth memory) chips used in Nvidia's AI accelerators, making it a primary beneficiary of the GPU buildout. The Nasdaq listing gives the company direct access to US capital markets and deeper integration with the American AI ecosystem — at a moment when the US is tightening semiconductor controls on China while loosening them for allies like the UAE.
sections.whyItMatters: SK Hynix's US listing underscores how AI demand is reshaping the semiconductor value chain. The company's HBM dominance makes it the pick-and-shovel play on Nvidia's GPU empire — and its decision to list in New York rather than remain solely in Seoul signals where the capital and strategic gravity of the AI chip market now sits.

sections.heading: Vietnam's Semiconductor Ambitions and the Critical Minerals Play
sections.body:
A Diplomat analysis details Vietnam's increasingly assertive semiconductor strategy. Fresh off its World Bank reclassification as an upper-middle-income country, Hanoi has launched what it calls the 'C = SET +1' formula — aiming to build specialization (S), electronics (E), and talent (T) as the preferred alternative to China for chip investment. Targets include 100 chip design companies, one fabrication facility, and 10 packaging and testing plants by 2030, scaling to 300 design firms and three fabs by 2050.

The government has committed to training 50,000 semiconductor engineers by 2030 with a $1 billion budget, launched a $100 million venture capital fund modeled on Israel's Yozma program, and plans to increase science and technology spending to 3-4% of the state budget. Vietnam is also positioning itself as a source of domestic rare earth minerals — critical inputs for chip manufacturing — as part of a broader effort to offer end-to-end supply chain resilience to investors seeking China diversification.

The challenge remains execution: Vietnam historically spent only 0.7% of its state budget on S&T, and the 'two-speed economy' — where foreign firms in Vietnam have not effectively upskilled local workers and firms — has persisted. Whether the new industrial policy overcomes that gap remains an open question.
sections.whyItMatters: Vietnam is making the most ambitious semiconductor play in Southeast Asia, combining rare earth access, workforce training, and venture capital to position itself as the 'plus one' in the global chip supply chain. If Hanoi delivers, it could reshape the geographic concentration risk that currently makes Taiwan and Korea irreplaceable.

bottomLine: This week the US opened a new front in its AI chip diplomacy by granting the UAE near-unrestricted access to Nvidia GPUs, rewarding a military ally while simultaneously failing to prevent frontier AI models from reaching Pentagon-blacklisted Chinese firms via Singapore. China, meanwhile, demonstrated it can now recover reusable orbital rockets — the capability that gave SpaceX and by extension the US its launch cadence advantage. Leaked military slides from a China-Russia forum revealed detailed plans for destroying Starlink satellites and building a countervailing space alliance. And the EU moved to regulate not just what Meta publishes, but how its products are designed, in the most significant DSA enforcement action to date. The connective thread: technology sovereignty is being claimed, contested, and regulated through every available channel — export controls, orbital real estate, platform architecture, and capital markets alike.
sources: Reuters, Arabian Business, 9to5Mac, Financial Times, WION, CryptoBriefing, Ars Technica, SpaceNews, Kyiv Post, The Insider, Der Spiegel, Le Monde, MSN, The Diplomat, Japan Times, Electronics Weekly, Dark Reading, Microsoft Security Blog
